---
title: "Ethan's Lecture 3 Post"
author: "Ethan W."
date: 2025-02-16
categories: [news, code, analysis]
image: "image.jpg"
---

##The Basic Geom Functions

- Bar charts use geom_bar();
- Line charts use geom_line();
- Boxplots use the geom_boxplot();
- Scatterplots use the geom_point();
- Fitted lines use the geom_smooth()


#Essential Geom Grammar

##Differentiation of categoricals

###Facet Wrap
- 
```{r}
ggplot(data =dataframe) + 
  geom_point(mapping = 
               aes(x = x_var , 
                   y = y_var,) )+
  facet_wrap( ~ cat_var, row ~ column)

```





```{r}
ggplot(data = dataframe) + 
  geom_point(mapping = 
               aes(x = x_var,
                   y = y_var,
                   shape = cat_var)
    ###can be done with color, alpha, and size too         
```


###The shapes of r 

image: "r shapes.jpg"

#Filtering Categoricals

```{r}
ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = 
         aes(x = x_var,
             y = y_var))+
  geom_point(mapping =
               aes(color = class),
                )
```

#Lecture 4

- ggthemes and hrbr themes package
- Its possible to use different aesthetic mappings
- ggsave() can be used to save a output, 
--ie. ggsave("name.pdf, plot = object)

#Classwork 4:

- WHen you do the cat variable = (shape,color,etc.) in the ggplot itself it will apply it to both items. When you want it for just one, just do it for that specific geom_*
-- It still needs to be in the aes() for both scenarios
- You can control whether the legend is there with show.legend. 
- You can merge two dataframes by using binding. Left bind puts higher precedence on the left dataframe.
